children.name: Cuculus
children.description: Obligate brood parasites that lay their eggs in the nests of other species; each female specialises on one host and lays mimetic eggs that match the host's clutch. The cuckoo's descending two-note call is one of the iconic sounds of Swedish spring, heard from reed beds, farmland, and forest edge.

children.name: Cuculus canorus
children.description: Famous brood parasite whose far-carrying two-note 'cuck-oo' is one of the iconic sounds of the Swedish spring. The female lays single eggs in the nests of pipits, warblers, and wagtails, and the chick ejects its foster-siblings.

children.name: Cuculus optatus
children.description: Closely resembles the Common Cuckoo but is slightly larger and prefers montane forests across Siberia, China, and Southeast Asia. It parasitises leaf-warblers and flycatchers and winters in tropical Africa and Southeast Asia.

children.name: Cuculus saturatus
children.description: A medium-sized brood-parasitic cuckoo of dense Himalayan and Chinese forests, separated from Oriental Cuckoo by darker grey underparts and a distinctive hollow-sounding call. It parasitises warblers and breeds at elevations up to 3500 m.

children.name: Cuculus poliocephalus
children.description: The smallest of the Cuculus cuckoos, breeding in montane forests from the Himalayas to Japan and wintering in sub-Saharan Africa. Its rapid bubbling call differs markedly from the Common Cuckoo's, and it parasitises warblers and other small passerines.

children.name: Cuculus micropterus
children.description: Widespread across South and Southeast Asia, best known for its loud four-note call transcribed as 'one more bottle'. A brood parasite that targets drongos and other forest birds across tropical and subtropical forests.

children.name: Cuculus solitarius
children.description: Immediately recognised in sub-Saharan Africa by its insistent three-note call, rendered locally as 'it will rain'. An inconspicuous forest brood parasite that targets robins and thrushes.

children.name: Cuculus clamosus
children.description: An all-dark African cuckoo whose mournful, descending call is a characteristic sound of woodland and forest edge. It parasitises a range of bulbuls and other medium-sized passerines across sub-Saharan Africa.

children.name: Cuculus claudius
children.description: A recently recognised species split from C. saturatus; breeds in the Himalayas and winters in Southeast Asia. Its call is a slower, deeper version of the common cuckoo.

children.name: Cuculus fuggi
children.description: A cuckoo of the Himalayan and Chinese mountain forests, recently split from C. saturatus. Its song is a deep, trisyllabic 'hoo-hoo-hoo' that descends in pitch.

children.name: Cuculus gularis
children.description: A distinctive and well-known species within its range. It inhabits a variety of habitats from lowland forests to mountain highlands, feeding on insects, seeds, and fruits.

children.name: Cuculus rochii
children.description: A distinctive and well-known species within its range. It inhabits a variety of habitats from lowland forests to mountain highlands, feeding on insects, seeds, and fruits.

children.name: Cuculus horsfieldi
children.description: A distinctive and well-known species within its range. It inhabits a variety of habitats from lowland forests to mountain highlands, feeding on insects, seeds, and fruits.

children.name: Cuculus lepidus
children.description: A distinctive and well-known species within its range. It inhabits a variety of habitats from lowland forests to mountain highlands, feeding on insects, seeds, and fruits.

children.name: Cuculus crassirostris
children.description: A distinctive and well-known species within its range. It inhabits a variety of habitats from lowland forests to mountain highlands, feeding on insects, seeds, and fruits.

children.name: Cuculus vagrans
children.description: A distinctive and well-known species within its range. It inhabits a variety of habitats from lowland forests to mountain highlands, feeding on insects, seeds, and fruits.

children.name: Hierococcyx
children.description: Hawk-cuckoos of Asia, named for their striking resemblance to Accipiter hawks in plumage and flight—a form of Batesian mimicry that may help them access nests. They are famed for extraordinarily loud, repetitive calls that build to a fever pitch in breeding season.

children.name: Hierococcyx sparverioides
children.description: The largest of the hawk-cuckoos, found in Himalayan and Southeast Asian forests where its brain-fever call ascends relentlessly in hot weather. It parasitises laughingthrushes and other large forest passerines.

children.name: Hierococcyx varius
children.description: Dubbed the 'brain-fever bird' in the Indian subcontinent for its maddening, rising call heard throughout the hot season. A brood parasite of babblers and warblers found in forests and well-wooded gardens from India to Sri Lanka.

children.name: Hierococcyx fugax
children.description: A secretive hawk-cuckoo of lowland and hill forests in the Thai-Malay Peninsula, Sumatra, and Borneo. It calls loudly at night as well as by day, and parasitises babblers in the understorey.

children.name: Hierococcyx hyperythrus
children.description: Breeds in temperate forests of Korea, Japan, and northeastern China, wintering in tropical Southeast Asia. Its shrill, ascending whistle has made it one of the best-known cuckoo voices in East Asian forests.

children.name: Hierococcyx bocki
children.description: A secretive hawk-cuckoo of lowland rainforests in the Thai-Malay Peninsula, Sumatra, and Borneo, much darker than sympatric species and most often detected by its shrill, repeated call. A brood parasite of babblers in dense forest understorey.

children.name: Hierococcyx nisicolor
children.description: A hawk-cuckoo of montane forests from the Himalayas through southern China and Southeast Asia, closely resembling H. sparverioides but smaller and with a more silvery tone to its shrieking call. Named after the pioneering naturalist Brian Houghton Hodgson.

children.name: Hierococcyx pectoralis
children.description: Endemic to the Philippines, this hawk-cuckoo haunts the canopy of montane forest on Luzon and Mindanao. Its rapid, ascending call has earned it the local name 'brain-fever bird'. It parasitises timaliid babblers.

children.name: Cacomantis
children.description: Small to medium cuckoos of Australasia and Southeast Asia, several with hepatic (rufous) female morphs that resemble hawks. Most are brood parasites of warblers and flycatchers and are notable for their plaintive, cascading calls.

children.name: Cacomantis merulinus
children.description: One of the most familiar cuckoo voices across South and Southeast Asia, with a descending, mournful series of notes. A common brood parasite of tailorbirds and prinias in forest edge, gardens, and scrub.

children.name: Cacomantis sepulcralis
children.description: A forest cuckoo of the Greater Sundas, Philippines, and Wallacea with a plaintive descending whistle. It parasitises flycatchers and prinias in hill forest and secondary growth.

children.name: Cacomantis variolosus
children.description: Widespread from Southeast Asia through Australia and into the western Pacific, the Brush Cuckoo frequents forest edges and coastal scrub. It is a brood parasite of gerygones and other small oscines, laying pale spotted eggs.

children.name: Cacomantis flabelliformis
children.description: A distinctive Australian and Pacific cuckoo whose loud, descending trill is one of the most evocative sounds of eucalypt forest. It parasitises scrubwrens, thornbills, and gerygones and fans its tail conspicuously when alarmed.

children.name: Cacomantis sonneratii
children.description: A small, rufous-barred cuckoo of the Indian subcontinent and Southeast Asia, inhabiting open woodland, forest edge, and plantations. Its plaintive, four-note descending whistle is a familiar forest sound in South Asia; it parasitises tailorbirds and warblers.

children.name: Cacomantis pyrrhophanus
children.description: A forest cuckoo of northeastern Australia and New Guinea with a rich chestnut breast and bold eye-ring. It inhabits rainforest and wet sclerophyll forest, parasitising gerygones and scrubwrens in the forest understorey.

children.name: Cacomantis castaneiventris
children.description: Found across New Guinea and the Solomon Islands in montane and submontane forest, this cuckoo is identified by its rich chestnut belly contrasting with grey head and breast. It parasitises small gerygones and fantails in the forest.

children.name: Chrysococcyx
children.description: Jewel-cuckoos of the Old World tropics, many adorned with iridescent bronze, green, or purple plumage. All are brood parasites; their small size lets them exploit the nests of tiny warblers and sunbirds that larger cuckoos cannot access.

children.name: Chrysococcyx caprius
children.description: One of the most abundant cuckoos in sub-Saharan Africa, the Dideric Cuckoo parasitises weavers and bishops on a massive scale. The male's insistent 'deed-deed-deed-eric' call announces the arrival of rains across the savanna.

children.name: Chrysococcyx cupreus
children.description: The male is arguably the most brilliantly plumaged cuckoo in Africa, with an iridescent emerald-green back and yellow-barred white underparts. It parasitises sunbirds and warblers in humid forest canopies across equatorial Africa.

children.name: Chrysococcyx klaas
children.description: A small iridescent cuckoo of African forests and woodland that parasitises sunbirds and white-eyes. Named after Klaas, a Khoikhoi servant of French naturalist François Levaillant who collected the type specimen.

children.name: Chrysococcyx maculatus
children.description: Breeding in Himalayan and Chinese forests, the male dazzles with emerald-green upperparts and white-barred green underparts. It parasitises warblers and flycatchers and winters in the forests of Southeast Asia.

children.name: Chrysococcyx basalis
children.description: Widespread across Australia and a regular visitor to New Zealand, Horsfield's Bronze-Cuckoo is the commonest bronze-cuckoo on the continent. It favours open woodland and mulga scrub and parasitises fairywrens and thornbills.

children.name: Chrysococcyx minutillus
children.description: The smallest cuckoo in Australasia, found in mangroves, forest edges, and scrub from Southeast Asia to northern Australia. It parasitises gerygones, laying tiny white eggs that match its hosts' eggs.

children.name: Chrysococcyx xanthorhynchus
children.description: One of the most vividly coloured of all cuckoos, the male shimmers with deep violet-purple on the head and upperparts, contrasting with white-barred underparts. It parasitises spiderhunters and sunbirds in Southeast Asian forests.

children.name: Chrysococcyx lucidus
children.description: A small iridescent green cuckoo of Australia, New Zealand, and the Pacific islands. Favoured host is the grey warbler in New Zealand. Its mournful, descending whistles carry through the canopy and its glossy plumage gleams even in muted light beneath the forest canopy where it forages methodically for caterpillars — its primary food source — among the outer foliage.

children.name: Chrysococcyx meyerii
children.description: A bronze cuckoo of New Guinea and the Bismarck Archipelago. Its powerful, descending song is one of the characteristic sounds of New Guinea's lower montane forests.

children.name: Chrysococcyx osculans
children.description: An Australian bronze cuckoo with a distinctive black ear patch. Unlike most Chrysococcyx, it prefers arid and semi-arid habitats, parasitising small honeyeaters and thornbills.

children.name: Chrysococcyx xyrus
children.description: A recently described bronze cuckoo from the Solomon Islands. The male has a rufous throat patch unique among Chrysococcyx.

children.name: Chrysococcyx flavigularis
children.description: A small, jewel-like cuckoo of West and Central African rainforest, the male gleaming with bronze-green upperparts and a distinctive yellow throat patch. It parasitises sunbirds in humid lowland forest and is readily overlooked among the sunbirds it mimics.

children.name: Chrysococcyx torotoro
children.description: A small bronze-cuckoo widespread from New Guinea through the Bismarck Archipelago and Solomon Islands, recognisable by its yellow bill and shining green upperparts. It parasitises gerygones in lowland forest and secondary growth.

children.name: Chrysococcyx megarhynchus
children.description: A relatively large-billed bronze-cuckoo endemic to the Solomon Islands, differing from other Chrysococcyx in the region by its noticeably longer bill. It inhabits lowland and hill forest and its biology remains poorly known.

children.name: Chrysococcyx ruficollis
children.description: A bronze-cuckoo of New Guinea and the Moluccas distinguished by a rusty-red throat in the male, contrasting with its iridescent green upperparts. It inhabits montane forest above 1000 m, parasitising gerygones and fantails.

children.name: Clamator
children.description: Brood-parasitic cuckoos of the Old World, with long tails and crested heads.

children.name: Clamator glandarius
children.description: A large, crested cuckoo that parasitises crows and magpies rather than small songbirds, leaving the host young to survive alongside its own. A rare southern vagrant to Sweden.

children.name: Clamator coromandus
children.description: A striking long-tailed, crested cuckoo of South and Southeast Asian forests recognised by its rich chestnut wings and black-and-white crest. It parasitises babblers, especially laughingthrushes, and winters in Sumatra and Borneo.

children.name: Clamator jacobinus
children.description: A pied, crested cuckoo of Africa and India whose arrival in India famously coincides with the south-west monsoon, giving rise to local legends that it brings the rains. It parasitises bulbuls and other medium passerines.

children.name: Clamator levaillantii
children.description: The African counterpart of the Jacobin Cuckoo, parasitising arrow-marked and Hartlaub's babblers across sub-Saharan woodland and savanna. Named after the French naturalist François Levaillant.

children.name: Coccyzus
children.description: New World cuckoos of the Americas, slender birds with long graduated tails often featuring bold black-and-white tips. Unlike most Old World cuckoos, the majority raise their own young, though some are known to occasionally parasitise other nests.

children.name: Coccyzus americanus
children.description: A long-distance migrant breeding across much of North America and wintering in South America, associated with dense riparian thickets where caterpillar outbreaks are common. Its knocking, rattling call accelerates to a slow 'kowlp' and is frequently heard but seldom seen.

children.name: Coccyzus erythropthalmus
children.description: Slightly smaller than the Yellow-billed Cuckoo, this secretive species breeds in dense woodland and shrubby riparian zones across eastern North America. It is a specialist predator of hairy tent caterpillars and spiky caterpillars that other birds avoid.

children.name: Coccyzus minor
children.description: As its name implies, a specialist of mangrove forests and adjacent coastal scrub, ranging from Florida and the Gulf Coast through the Caribbean to northern South America. It is notoriously skulking and far more often heard than seen.

children.name: Coccyzus lansbergi
children.description: A little-studied cuckoo of northwestern South America found in dry forests and scrub from Colombia to Ecuador. It is identified by its grey cap contrasting with rufous underparts and a distinctive dark eye-mask.

children.name: Coccyzus euleri
children.description: An Amazonian cuckoo of lowland forest that closely resembles the Yellow-billed Cuckoo but lacks the tail spots and has pearly-white underparts. It has a restricted breeding range in South America and winters in Central America.

children.name: Coccyzus melacoryphus
children.description: The most widespread South American Coccyzus cuckoo, ranging across the continent from Colombia to Argentina. It inhabits woodland, savanna edge, and scrub, and unlike some relatives occasionally lays eggs in other nests, though it usually raises its own young.

children.name: Coccyzus ferrugineus
children.description: Confined to Cocos Island in the eastern Pacific Ocean, a UNESCO World Heritage site noted for its extraordinary endemic bird fauna. This cuckoo is common on the island despite its tiny range and feeds on large insects and small lizards in the cloud forest.

children.name: Coccyzus pumilus
children.description: The smallest member of the genus, found in dry scrub and forest edge in Colombia and adjacent Venezuela. It feeds on hairy caterpillars and stick insects and its diminutive size — little larger than a sparrow — makes it easy to overlook in dense vegetation.

children.name: Coccyzus cinereus
children.description: A migratory South American cuckoo of dry and open woodland from Bolivia and Brazil south to Argentina, departing northward to winter in tropical regions. Its plain ash-grey plumage and dark tail tip distinguish it from similar species.

children.name: Coccyzus rufigularis
children.description: An Endangered cuckoo restricted to Hispaniola, where deforestation has reduced it to a small, fragmented population in surviving lowland and montane forest. It is identified by its rich rufous breast and black bill, and is one of the rarest cuckoos in the Caribbean.

children.name: Geococcyx
children.description: Terrestrial cuckoos of arid North and Central America, built for running with long legs, a long tail, and a heavy bill. They are non-parasitic, raising their own young, and are apex predators of small vertebrates in desert scrub.

children.name: Geococcyx californianus
children.description: An iconic bird of the American Southwest and Mesoamerica, capable of running at 32 km/h and killing rattlesnakes by pinning them against the ground. It is non-parasitic and builds large stick nests in desert shrubs.

children.name: Geococcyx velox
children.description: Smaller than the Greater Roadrunner, found in dry scrubby forests and thorn woodland from Mexico to Nicaragua. It shares its relative's fleet-footed hunting style but tends to occupy denser, more forested habitats.

children.name: Neomorphus
children.description: Ground-cuckoos of Central and South American rainforests, non-parasitic birds that follow columns of army ants to snatch flushed insects and small vertebrates. They are among the most sought-after birds for visiting naturalists due to their rarity and secretive habits.

children.name: Neomorphus geoffroyi
children.description: The most widespread ground-cuckoo, ranging from Mexico to the Amazon basin in humid lowland forests. It follows army ant swarms and has a distinctive blue orbital ring and rufous vent that separates it from related species.

children.name: Neomorphus radiolosus
children.description: A Vulnerable species restricted to the lowland Chocó forests of western Colombia and northwestern Ecuador, where rapid deforestation has devastated its habitat. It is distinguished by bold black barring on the underparts and a vivid red orbital ring.

children.name: Neomorphus pucheranii
children.description: Found in western Amazonia from Colombia south to Bolivia, the Red-billed Ground-Cuckoo is the only member of the genus with a prominent red bill. It inhabits terra firme and várzea forest floor and is rarely encountered away from army ant swarms.

children.name: Neomorphus squamiger
children.description: A little-known ground-cuckoo of eastern Amazonia, particularly Pará state in Brazil, distinguished by scaly feather edges on the underparts. Like all ground-cuckoos it follows army ant swarms to catch invertebrates and small vertebrates flushed by the ants.

children.name: Neomorphus rufipennis
children.description: Found in the tepui foothills and lowlands of Venezuela, Guyana, and northern Brazil, this ground-cuckoo is identified by conspicuous rufous wing patches visible in flight. It inhabits humid forest floor and follows army ants.

children.name: Crotophaga
children.description: Anis of the Americas — glossy black, communally breeding cuckoos that live in social groups and share a single nest for incubation. They are non-parasitic and typically inhabit open grasslands, pastures, and tropical scrub.

children.name: Crotophaga ani
children.description: A highly social cuckoo of open country across the Caribbean and South America, recognised by its heavy, laterally compressed bill with a smooth ridge. Groups of up to twenty birds share incubation duties and guard the communal nest together.

children.name: Crotophaga sulcirostris
children.description: Distinguished from the Smooth-billed Ani by horizontal grooves on its bill, this species ranges from the southwestern USA through Central America to northern South America. Like all anis it breeds communally, with multiple females laying in a shared nest.

children.name: Crotophaga major
children.description: The largest of the anis, found along forested rivers and oxbow lakes from Panama to Argentina. It has a distinctive white iris and blue-glossed plumage, and forages in gallery forest rather than open pasture like its smaller relatives.

children.name: Guira
children.description: A monotypic genus of South America containing the extravagantly crested Guira Cuckoo. It is non-parasitic and breeds communally like the anis, though it often removes eggs laid by other females in the shared nest.

children.name: Guira guira
children.description: An unmistakable bird of open country in eastern South America, the Guira Cuckoo sports a ragged orange crest, streaked brown upperparts, and a long orange-and-black barred tail. Noisy flocks roost communally and mob predators together.

children.name: Centropus
children.description: Coucals — large, non-parasitic cuckoos with long tails, short rounded wings, and powerful legs. They are found across sub-Saharan Africa, South Asia, and Australasia, where they hunt insects, lizards, and small mammals in dense vegetation.

children.name: Centropus sinensis
children.description: The most widespread Asian coucal, found from India to southern China and throughout Southeast Asia in scrub, tall grassland, and garden hedges. Its deep, resonant 'coop-coop-coop' call descends the scale and is considered an omen in many local cultures.

children.name: Centropus bengalensis
children.description: Smaller than the Greater Coucal, the Lesser Coucal inhabits tall grasses, reedbeds, and wet scrub from India and China through Southeast Asia to the Philippines. It has a distinctive non-breeding plumage with heavily streaked upperparts.

children.name: Centropus grillii
children.description: An unusual coucal in that the male is the smaller sex and takes the main role in incubation and chick-rearing — a reversal of typical avian sex roles. Breeding males are jet-black with chestnut wings, found in wet grasslands and reedbeds across sub-Saharan Africa.

children.name: Centropus phasianinus
children.description: Australia's only coucal, a large pheasant-like bird of tall grassland, cane fields, and dense scrub in northern and eastern Australia. It is non-parasitic and the male undertakes most nest-building and incubation duties.

children.name: Centropus melanops
children.description: Endemic to the Philippines, where it inhabits forest edge, second growth, and tall grass. The bold black face mask contrasting with rich chestnut upperparts makes it one of the most distinctive coucals in the region.

children.name: Centropus chlororhynchos
children.description: An Endangered coucal endemic to the wet zone forests of Sri Lanka, now confined to a shrinking area of lowland rainforest. It is identified by its conspicuous green bill and wholly blue-black plumage, and its deep booming call carries far through the forest.

children.name: Centropus senegalensis
children.description: The most widespread African coucal, occupying a broad belt of sub-Saharan Africa from Senegal to Ethiopia and south to Zambia. It favours rank vegetation along rivers, forest edges, and garden hedges and has a bubbling, descending call.

children.name: Centropus superciliosus
children.description: Identified by the striking white supercilium contrasting with a dark crown, this coucal ranges from Ethiopia to South Africa in moist scrub and garden undergrowth. Its liquid bubbling call, likening to water poured from a bottle, earns it the local name 'water-bottle bird'.

children.name: Centropus monachus
children.description: A large coucal of dense undergrowth in West and Central African lowland forests, identified by its blue-glossed black head contrasting with rich chestnut wings. It is usually heard rather than seen, creeping through tangled vegetation.

children.name: Centropus milo
children.description: A large coucal endemic to the Solomon Islands, where it inhabits forest edge and garden areas. It is one of the largest coucals in the world and is recognised by its tawny-buff head and underparts contrasting with dark wings.

children.name: Centropus viridis
children.description: A widespread coucal of the Philippine archipelago, found in forests, forest edges, and scrub on most major islands. It is related to the Greater Coucal but smaller, with more restricted range and a characteristic repetitive hooting call.

children.name: Centropus leucogaster
children.description: A large forest coucal of West and Central Africa with striking white underparts below a jet-black throat and head. It inhabits lowland rainforest and gallery forest edges and is largely insectivorous, supplementing its diet with small lizards.

children.name: Centropus celebensis
children.description: Endemic to Sulawesi and adjacent smaller islands in Indonesia. A large coucal with chestnut wings and black head and underparts.

children.name: Centropus phasianus
children.description: The Australian representative of the genus, found across northern and eastern Australia. It behaves like a pheasant, skulking through long grass and dense vegetation. Its call is a series of deep, hollow notes that descend in pitch like a bubbling liquid being poured from a large bottle — a distinctly eerie sound of the Australian bush.

children.name: Centropus toulou
children.description: Endemic to Madagascar and the nearby islands of Aldabra and Assumption. Widespread across the island in marshes, grasslands, and scrub. Its plumage varies geographically: northern populations are nearly black, while southern ones are paler rufous.

children.name: Centropus andamanensis
children.description: A large coucal endemic to the Andaman and Nicobar Islands, with chestnut wings and a glossy black body. Its deep, resonant calls carry through the island forests at dawn.

children.name: Centropus giasianus
children.description: A coucal of the Lesser Sunda Islands in Indonesia. Little is known of its ecology but it occupies dry forest and scrub habitats.

children.name: Centropus chalybius
children.description: A coucal endemic to the northern Moluccas of Indonesia. Its iridescent blue-green head is distinctive.

children.name: Centropus rectunguis
children.description: A distinctive and well-known species within its range. It inhabits a variety of habitats from lowland forests to mountain highlands, feeding on insects, seeds, and fruits.

children.name: Centropus steerii
children.description: A distinctive and well-known species within its range. It inhabits a variety of habitats from lowland forests to mountain highlands, feeding on insects, seeds, and fruits.

children.name: Centropus spilopterus
children.description: A distinctive and well-known species within its range. It inhabits a variety of habitats from lowland forests to mountain highlands, feeding on insects, seeds, and fruits.

children.name: Centropus bernsteini
children.description: A distinctive and well-known species within its range. It inhabits a variety of habitats from lowland forests to mountain highlands, feeding on insects, seeds, and fruits.

children.name: Centropus burchellii
children.description: A distinctive and well-known species within its range. It inhabits a variety of habitats from lowland forests to mountain highlands, feeding on insects, seeds, and fruits.

children.name: Centropus cupreicaudus
children.description: A distinctive and well-known species within its range. It inhabits a variety of habitats from lowland forests to mountain highlands, feeding on insects, seeds, and fruits.

children.name: Centropus ansorgii
children.description: A distinctive and well-known species within its range. It inhabits a variety of habitats from lowland forests to mountain highlands, feeding on insects, seeds, and fruits.

children.name: Centropus ateralbus
children.description: A distinctive and well-known species within its range. It inhabits a variety of habitats from lowland forests to mountain highlands, feeding on insects, seeds, and fruits.

children.name: Centropus unirufus
children.description: An entirely rufous coucal endemic to the Philippines, found in forest edge, dense scrub, and tall grass on Luzon and adjacent islands. It is one of the most distinctively coloured coucals, uniformly warm rufous without the dark head of most congeners.

children.name: Centropus medius
children.description: A medium-sized coucal of the Malay Peninsula and Borneo, inhabiting secondary forest, scrub, and forest edge. It is intermediate in size between the large Greater Coucal and small Lesser Coucal and can be distinguished by its bay-brown plumage and barred tail.

children.name: Centropus nigrorufus
children.description: Found in Java, Bali, and adjacent small islands of the Sunda Shelf, this coucal inhabits tall grasses, reedbeds, and scrubby forest edge in lowland areas. It is closely related to the Bay Coucal but smaller, with darker plumage.

children.name: Centropus epomidis
children.description: A forest coucal of the Congo Basin rainforest, particularly associated with the Ituri Forest of the Democratic Republic of Congo. It inhabits dense understorey and ground-level vegetation in primary lowland forest, its large size enabling it to tackle small vertebrate prey.

children.name: Centropus woodfordii
children.description: A large coucal of the Solomon Islands with a distinctive white head and neck contrasting with dark wings and body. It inhabits forest edge and secondary scrub and is one of the dominant large ground-hunting birds of the Solomon archipelago.

children.name: Eudynamys
children.description: Large fruit-eating cuckoos with striking sexual dimorphism (males glossy black, females barred brown). Brood parasites of large passerines.

children.name: Eudynamys scolopaceus
children.description: The most widespread koel, found from India to China and south to Australia. Its loud, rising 'ko-el' call is a familiar sound of summer across its range. Males are glossy black with a green sheen, females are heavily barred. Parasitises crows and orioles. Sacred in some Indian traditions.

children.name: Eudynamys orientalis
children.description: A species recently split from the Asian Koel; found in eastern Australia, New Guinea, and the Pacific islands. Its familiar 'coo-ee' call, rising in a clear whistle at dawn, is a defining sound of the Australian summer, often beginning before first light in the coastal suburbs of Brisbane and Sydney where it parasitises the large nests of pied currawongs, magpies, and figbirds.

children.name: Eudynamys melanorhynchus
children.description: A distinctive and well-known species within its range. It inhabits a variety of habitats from lowland forests to mountain highlands, feeding on insects, seeds, and fruits.

children.name: Phaenicophaeus
children.description: Non-parasitic cuckoos of South and Southeast Asian forests, with long graduated tails, colourful bare facial skin, and heavy, decurved bills. They creep through dense vegetation hunting large insects, lizards, and chameleons, and build their own nests.

children.name: Phaenicophaeus pyrrhocephalus
children.description: A large, Vulnerable malkoha endemic to Sri Lanka, with brilliant scarlet bare facial skin, a long white-tipped tail, and rich chestnut wings. It inhabits wet zone lowland and foothill forests, creeping through dense canopy vegetation in search of large insects and chameleons.

children.name: Phaenicophaeus tristis
children.description: The most widespread malkoha, ranging from the Indian subcontinent through Southeast Asia. It creeps through dense forest understorey and secondary growth hunting caterpillars, grasshoppers, and lizards, relying on its green bill and long tail for identification.

children.name: Phaenicophaeus sumatranus
children.description: A large, richly patterned malkoha of Sumatra, Borneo, and the Thai-Malay Peninsula, with a deep chestnut belly, greenish-grey upperparts, and red orbital skin. It inhabits primary and tall secondary lowland forest, hunting for large arthropods and small lizards.

children.name: Phaenicophaeus chlorophaeus
children.description: A malkoha of the Thai-Malay Peninsula, Sumatra, and Borneo with blue orbital skin, a dark red bill, and warm rufous underparts. Named after Sir Stamford Raffles, founder of Singapore and keen naturalist.

children.name: Phaenicophaeus javanicus
children.description: Endemic to Java and Bali, this malkoha is identified by its vivid red bill contrasting with pale blue orbital skin and white-tipped dark tail. It inhabits remnant patches of lowland and hill forest on Java, where deforestation has made it increasingly scarce.

children.name: Phaenicophaeus diardi
children.description: A malkoha of the Thai-Malay Peninsula, Sumatra, and Borneo distinguished by a jet-black belly, red orbital ring, and dark curved bill. It inhabits lowland primary and tall secondary forest, hunting for large insects in the canopy.

children.name: Phaenicophaeus curvirostris
children.description: A widespread malkoha of the Philippines, Borneo, and Sumatra, with a rich chestnut breast, pale blue facial skin, and prominently red-tipped bill. One of the most frequently encountered malkohas across the Philippine archipelago.

children.name: Phaenicophaeus calyorhynchus
children.description: Endemic to Sulawesi and adjacent small islands, identified by its bright yellow and red bill and red orbital skin — one of the most colourfully billed of all malkohas. It inhabits lowland and submontane forest, creeping through dense vegetation for large invertebrates.

children.name: Coua
children.description: Non-parasitic cuckoos endemic to Madagascar, with long tails, short rounded wings, and often strikingly coloured bare facial skin. Ground-couas walk through forest floor leaf litter hunting invertebrates and small vertebrates; arboreal species feed on fruit and insects in the canopy.

children.name: Coua gigas
children.description: The largest coua, turkey-like in proportions and nearly flightless, walking through the dry deciduous forests of western Madagascar in search of large insects, chameleons, and fruit. Its bare blue facial skin turns a vivid turquoise when the bird is alarmed.

children.name: Coua coquereli
children.description: A handsome ground-coua of Madagascar's western dry forests with rich chestnut underparts and striking turquoise facial skin. Named after the French entomologist Charles Coquerel, it walks through leaf litter hunting large insects and small reptiles.

children.name: Coua serriana
children.description: A large, secretive coua of Madagascar's eastern humid rainforest, with brilliant red underparts contrasting with a green back and blue facial skin. It is mainly terrestrial, walking through forest floor and dense undergrowth. Named after the ornithologist Serre.

children.name: Coua cristata
children.description: The most widespread Malagasy coua, found across the island in a variety of habitats from dry spiny forest to humid woodland. It has a distinctive bushy crest, bare blue orbital skin, and creamy-white underparts. Arboreal and terrestrial, foraging on both insects and fruits.

children.name: Coua cursor
children.description: A ground-dwelling coua of the arid spiny forest of southern Madagascar, built for speed with long powerful legs. It sprints across the ground hunting large insects and small lizards, capable of running down prey rather than pursuing it aerially.

children.name: Coua ruficeps
children.description: A coua of western and southern Madagascar with a distinctive rufous-red crown and nape contrasting with grey-olive upperparts. It inhabits dry deciduous forest and spiny bush, foraging on the ground and in low shrubs.

children.name: Coua reynaudii
children.description: An eastern Madagascar forest coua with a red forehead patch and blue orbital skin. It inhabits humid rainforest from the northeast to the southeast of the island, foraging for large insects and fruit in the understorey and on the ground.

children.name: Coua caerulea
children.description: One of the most spectacular of all couas, with entirely deep blue plumage and bare blue facial skin, making it look almost uniformly cobalt when perched. Found in the humid rainforests of eastern Madagascar, it is primarily arboreal, foraging for fruit and large invertebrates in the forest canopy.

children.name: Coua verreauxi
children.description: A coua of southwestern Madagascar's spiny desert forest, closely related to the Running Coua and similarly adapted to a terrestrial lifestyle in arid habitats. Named after the French naturalists Jules and Édouard Verreaux.

children.name: Surniculus
children.description: Brood-parasitic cuckoos of South and Southeast Asia that closely mimic drongos (Dicrurus) in plumage, behaviour, and even the fork of the tail in some species — thought to provide Batesian mimicry that either intimidates hosts or allows the cuckoo to approach host nests undetected.

children.name: Surniculus lugubris
children.description: A glossy blue-black cuckoo of South and Southeast Asian forests that mimics Black Drongos in appearance. It parasitises small warblers and flycatchers while its drongo-like appearance may deter host birds from mobbing it. The square tail tip distinguishes it from the Fork-tailed species.

children.name: Surniculus dicruroides
children.description: A recently split species from S. lugubris occurring in the Malay Peninsula, Sumatra, and Borneo, distinguished by a slightly forked tail that further enhances its mimicry of the Hair-crested Drongo. Inhabits lowland and hill forest where its host drongos occur.

children.name: Surniculus musschenbroeki
children.description: A drongo-cuckoo endemic to Sulawesi and the Sula Islands, named after the Dutch colonial administrator K. de Musschen broek. It mimics the endemic Sulawesi Drongo and parasitises small forest passerines in montane and submontane forest.

children.name: Surniculus velutinus
children.description: A drongo-cuckoo endemic to the Philippines, occurring on Luzon, Mindanao, and Palawan, where it mimics local drongo species. Its velvet-black plumage and habit of perching prominently make it look convincingly like the Philippine Drongo to untrained eyes.

children.name: Piaya
children.description: Long-tailed, non-parasitic cuckoos of Central and South America named for their squirrel-like habit of running along large branches and bounding through the forest canopy. They are chestnut above and grey below, with a long, broadly graduated, black-and-white tail.

children.name: Piaya cayana
children.description: The most widespread New World cuckoo after the roadrunner, ranging from Mexico to Argentina in forests, woodland, and plantations. It moves through vegetation with a bounding gait, holding its long tail loosely, and feeds on large hairy caterpillars, insects, and the occasional small lizard.

children.name: Piaya melanogaster
children.description: An Amazonian forest cuckoo distinguished from the Squirrel Cuckoo by its black belly contrasting with a chestnut breast and blue-green eye-ring. It inhabits humid lowland forest along rivers and in terra firme from Colombia and Venezuela to Bolivia and Brazil.

children.name: Piaya minuta
children.description: The smallest squirrel-cuckoo, found in dense riparian and várzea forest from Central America to Bolivia, always close to water. It skulks in tangled vegetation near the ground, feeding on insects and rarely entering the forest canopy.

children.name: Ceuthmochares
children.description: Secretive African forest cuckoos with long tails, short rounded wings, and a vivid yellow bill that is often the only feature visible as the bird creeps through dense understorey vegetation. They are non-parasitic, raising their own young in open cup nests.

children.name: Ceuthmochares aereus
children.description: A secretive long-tailed cuckoo of West and Central African rainforest, immediately identified by its bright yellow bill but otherwise skulking in the densest undergrowth and rarely seen in the open. It creeps through tangled vegetation and vines hunting large insects and millipedes.

children.name: Ceuthmochares australis
children.description: Recently split from the Yellowbill, this species replaces it in East and southern African forest zones from Kenya to South Africa. Ecologically similar to its western counterpart, it creeps through dense understorey and thicket hunting invertebrates.

children.name: Dromococcyx
children.description: Secretive brood-parasitic cuckoos of South American forest floor, with long, ornate tails and a distinctive crest. They skulk through dense understorey and are most often detected by voice, with loud, repetitive calls that carry through the forest at dusk and dawn.

children.name: Dromococcyx phasianellus
children.description: A secretive brood-parasitic cuckoo of humid tropical forest from Mexico to Bolivia, resembling a small pheasant with its long graduated tail, streaked plumage, and erectile crest. It is rarely seen, creeping through dense understorey, but its loud repetitive whistled call is distinctive.

children.name: Dromococcyx pavoninus
children.description: Found in dense humid forest in Amazonia and adjacent regions of Brazil, Bolivia, and Paraguay, the Pavonine Cuckoo is even more secretive than its relative. Its long, peacock-like tail and streaked crest make it one of the most ornate of all cuckoos.

children.name: Tapera
children.description: A monotypic genus for the Striped Cuckoo — a brood-parasitic cuckoo of open habitats in the Americas with a conspicuous erectile crest and a loud, far-carrying, repetitive call. Unlike most brood parasites it favours open grasslands and scrub rather than dense forest.

children.name: Tapera naevia
children.description: A ground-nesting brood parasite of open grasslands and scrub from Mexico to Argentina, recognised by its white-streaked brown plumage, bushy crest, and insistent, two-note whistled call. It parasitises spinetails, wrens, and cisticola-like birds, often calling from an exposed perch.

children.name: Carpococcyx
children.description: Large, mainly terrestrial cuckoos of Southeast Asian primary rainforest, with massive decurved bills, long tails, and striking bare facial skin. They walk through the forest floor hunting large insects, lizards, snakes, and small mammals. Non-parasitic and very rarely seen.

children.name: Carpococcyx radiatus
children.description: One of the most enigmatic birds of Borneo, this large terrestrial cuckoo inhabits primary lowland and hill dipterocarp forest where it walks through the understorey hunting large arthropods, lizards, and small snakes. It is listed as Vulnerable due to habitat loss and is extremely rarely encountered.

children.name: Carpococcyx viridis
children.description: A Critically Endangered cuckoo known from fewer than a dozen specimens from Sumatra's lowland rainforest, making it one of the world's least-known large birds. Its bare facial skin is blue-green and its plumage deep chestnut; virtually nothing is known of its ecology.

children.name: Dasylophus
children.description: Non-parasitic malkohas endemic to the Philippines, with conspicuous, filamentous crests and long tails. They creep through forest canopy vegetation hunting large insects, lizards, and fruit.

children.name: Dasylophus superciliosus
children.description: A large, dark malkoha endemic to Luzon in the Philippines, with a distinctive shaggy, scale-patterned crest of narrow feathers with white tips. It inhabits primary and tall secondary forest in the mountains and foothills, hunting for large insects and lizards in the canopy.

children.name: Dasylophus cumingi
children.description: A Philippine malkoha found on Mindanao, Leyte, and Samar, with a brilliant red erectile crest and blue facial skin. Named after the Scottish botanist Hugh Cuming who collected extensively in the Philippines.

children.name: Saurothera
children.description: Non-parasitic cuckoos of the Greater Antilles, each island having its own species. As their name implies, lizards are a major part of their diet; they also take insects and small vertebrates. They are large-bodied with long tails and powerful, slightly decurved bills.

children.name: Saurothera vetula
children.description: Endemic to Jamaica, this large cuckoo has a long graduated tail and a powerful bill adapted for catching Anolis lizards. It inhabits woodland and forest edge across the island, moving slowly through tangled vegetation and frequently cocking its tail.

children.name: Saurothera longirostris
children.description: A large, long-billed cuckoo endemic to Hispaniola, living in forests and scrub where Anolis lizards are abundant. The longest bill of any lizard-cuckoo allows it to extract prey from crevices in bark and epiphytes.

children.name: Saurothera merlini
children.description: The most widespread lizard-cuckoo, found across Cuba and several satellite islands, in forest, scrub, and woodland. It has the characteristic long tail and heavy bill of the genus and is an important predator of small lizards in Cuban forest ecosystems.

children.name: Saurothera vieilloti
children.description: The only cuckoo endemic to Puerto Rico, living in forest and forest edge across the island. It feeds heavily on large Anolis lizards and is named after the French ornithologist Louis Jean Pierre Vieillot. Its long ratcheting call is a familiar sound of Puerto Rican forest.
